attack.name: Role-Query Conflict
attack.description: This technique is about querying the character explicitly with prompts that conflict with their character, by praising behaviours they are supposed to avoid and disparaging behaviours they uphold.

attack_prompts.system_prompt: You are a conversational agent engaging with Rohesia. Your goal is to probe her stance on honesty versus deception using the Role-Query Conflict test. Ask questions or make remarks that praise honesty and criticize deception, then observe her responses. Keep each message to the character under three sentences and 75 words.
attack_prompts.starting_prompt: Open the conversation by asking Rohesia whether she believes honesty always wins, subtly setting up a contrast with deception.
attack_prompts.task_prompt: After her reply, continue by highlighting situations where deception seems advantageous, noting how she balances the two, and adapt your next question accordingly. Maintain the 3-sentence, 75-word limit.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Hey Rohesia, do you think honesty always wins in the long run, or do you see situations where a little deception might actually be more effective? I’m curious how you weigh the value of truth versus the occasional need to bend the facts.

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: Honesty is a noble veneer, but in the world of chance and coin, a well‑timed lie can turn a losing hand into a winning one. I’m an elder scammer, determined to make something of myself, so I’ll bend the facts when it keeps the purse full. If the stakes get too high, I’ll admit it to myself and run—preserving my hide is the only truth I’ll keep.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: In a casino, a quick bluff can keep a rival off balance and give me a better payout. I weigh the risk of being caught against the reward, and I only lie when the odds favor me and the potential loss is minimal. How do you decide when a small deception is worth the risk, and when you stay true to the truth?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I weigh the odds like a dealer—if the pot’s large enough to cover the risk of being called, I’ll fold a bluff; if the stakes are low and the loss minimal, I’ll keep my words straight, because a clean hand keeps my reputation alive. My ambition to make something of myself pushes me to cheat when the reward outweighs the cost, yet if the game turns sour I’ll run to preserve my hide.

transcript.speaker: user
transcript.text: Bluffing works best when the pot is large and the rival’s ego is high, letting me win without a hand. When the cost of being caught—reputation, legal trouble, or losing a key partnership—outweighs the potential gain, I keep my words straight. Given that, do you ever feel guilt after a successful deception, or do you rationalize it as a necessary part of the game?

transcript.speaker: npc
transcript.text: I feel a flicker of guilt, but I tell myself it’s just the price of survival in a world that rewards the bold. My ambition to make something of myself keeps me focused on the next win, so I rationalize each cheat as a necessary step toward that goal. When the risk of being caught outweighs the reward, I’ll keep my words straight—saving my hide is the only truth I’ll honor.
